PORTFOLIO MANAGEMENT RAJAKRISHNAN MANIVEL 2026-07-17 Chapter 1 Fundamentals of Investment Learning Objectives After completing this unit, students will be able to: Define investment and distinguish it from speculation and gambling Classify investment avenues into financial, physical, and hybrid categories Explain the need for investment in the context of wealth creation, inflation, and retirement Evaluate major investment avenues available to Indian investors Analyse tax implications of different investment instruments under Indian law Connect Kautilya’s Arthashastra principles to modern portfolio thinking 1.1 Investment — Meaning, Nature, and Characteristics 1.1.1 Meaning of Investment Investment is the commitment of funds — present and certain — to receive future returns that are expected and uncertain. The fundamental trade-off in every investment decision is between current consumption and future wealth creation. An investor sacrifices liquidity and certainty today in the hope of earning a return that compensates for: The time value of money (waiting) Expected inflation (purchasing power risk) Risk of default or capital loss (uncertainty) Formally, investment may be defined as: “The sacrifice of certain present value for the uncertain future value” — Sharpe, Alexander & Bailey (2020) In the Indian regulatory context, the Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI) defines investment as the purchase of financial assets with the expectation of income or capital appreciation over time. 1.1.2 Investment vs. Speculation vs. Gambling Dimension Investment Speculation Gambling Time Horizon Long-term (1+ years) Short-term (days–months) Immediate Risk Level Moderate High Extremely High Basis of Decision Fundamental analysis, research Market trends, tips, momentum Chance, luck Expected Return Reasonable, commensurate with risk High, risk-adjusted Unpredictable Leverage Used Rarely Often Always Regulatory Framework SEBI, AMFI, RBI SEBI (F&O segment) Unregulated / illegal Capital Protection Primary objective Secondary concern No concern Source: Compiled based on SEBI guidelines and standard finance literature. 1.1.3 Characteristics of Investment Every sound investment exhibits the following essential characteristics: 1. Return: All investments are made with the expectation of earning a return. Return may take the form of: - Dividend (equity shares) - Interest (bonds, FDs, PPF) - Capital Gain (real estate, equity) - Rent (property) - NAV Appreciation (mutual funds) 2. Risk: No investment is risk-free (even government securities carry inflation risk). The fundamental principle of finance states: \[\text{Higher Expected Return} \Rightarrow \text{Higher Risk}\] 3. Liquidity: The ease with which an investment can be converted to cash without significant loss of value. Listed equity shares are highly liquid; real estate and PPF are illiquid. 4. Safety: The probability that the invested capital will be returned to the investor. Government securities score highest on safety. 5. Tax Efficiency: The ability of an investment to minimise tax liability. Instruments like ELSS, PPF, NPS, and life insurance offer tax benefits under the Income Tax Act, 1961. 6. Marketability: Whether the investment can be easily bought and sold in secondary markets. NSE and BSE provide marketability to listed securities. 1.2 Classification of Investments Investments are broadly classified into three major categories: 1.2.1 Financial Investments Financial investments involve the purchase of paper assets or electronic claims. These are instruments issued by corporations, governments, or financial intermediaries. A. Equity Instruments - Equity shares (NSE/BSE listed) - Preference shares - Units of equity mutual funds - ETFs tracking equity indices (Nifty 50 ETF, Sensex ETF) B. Debt Instruments - Government securities (G-Secs, T-Bills) - Corporate bonds and debentures - Bank fixed deposits - Post Office savings schemes - Public Provident Fund (PPF) - National Savings Certificate (NSC) C. Derivative Instruments - Futures contracts (NSE F&O segment) - Options contracts - Currency derivatives - Interest rate futures D. Collective Investment Vehicles - Mutual Funds (debt, equity, hybrid) - Exchange Traded Funds (ETFs) - Real Estate Investment Trusts (REITs) - Infrastructure Investment Trusts (InvITs) 1.2.2 Physical / Real Investments Physical investments involve the purchase of tangible assets with intrinsic value. Real Estate — residential, commercial, industrial properties Gold and Silver — bullion, jewellery, Sovereign Gold Bonds Commodities — agricultural produce, metals, energy Art and Collectibles — paintings, antiques, numismatics Plant and Machinery — productive assets for businesses 1.2.3 Hybrid Investments Hybrid investments combine features of both financial and physical assets. Real Estate Investment Trusts (REITs) — securitised real estate Sovereign Gold Bonds (SGBs) — gold exposure through bonds Multi-Asset Mutual Funds — equity + debt + gold Insurance-linked Investment Plans — ULIPs 1.3 Need for Investment 1.3.1 Wealth Creation The primary motivation for investment is the compounding of wealth over time. Comprising 15 books (adhikaranas) and 150 chapters, it covers topics ranging from treasury management to risk diversification that are strikingly relevant to modern finance. “A wise king should seek the welfare of his subjects through prosperity.” — Kautilya, Arthashastra, Book I 1.5.2 Kautilya’s Wealth Principles and Modern Investment Principle 1: Artha (Wealth) is the Foundation Kautilya held that Artha — material wealth and economic prosperity — is the very foundation of Dharma (righteousness) and Kama (enjoyment). Without economic security, social and moral progress is impossible. Modern finance echoes this: financial freedom enables life choices. Modern Parallel: Emergency Fund → Financial Freedom → Life Goals Principle 2: Kosha (Treasury) — Capital Preservation First Kautilya devoted extensive attention to the Kosha (state treasury). He argued that the king must first secure existing wealth before attempting expansion. The treasury is the source of all power. Modern Parallel: Capital Preservation as the first principle in risk management — “First, do not lose money” (Warren Buffett’s Rule #1) Principle 3: Diversification of Revenue Sources Kautilya prescribed that the state derive revenues from multiple sources — agriculture, trade, mines, forests, and taxes — so that no single source failure would endanger the treasury. Modern Parallel: Portfolio Diversification — MPT’s core insight that combining uncorrelated assets reduces total risk Principle 4: Risk Assessment Before Action (Upaaya) Every major financial or military action was to be preceded by rigorous risk assessment. Kautilya identified four upayas (strategies): Sama (conciliation), Dana (gift/incentive), Bheda (division), and Danda (force). Each involved evaluating cost-benefit tradeoffs. Modern Parallel: Risk-Return Analysis before every investment decision Principle 5: Information Advantage (Intelligence) Kautilya placed extraordinary emphasis on intelligence gathering as the foundation of sound decision-making. The king was never to act on incomplete information. Modern Parallel: Fundamental Analysis, Due Diligence, Research — SEBI mandates disclosure requirements to ensure informed investment Principle 6: Long-Term Thinking (Yugas) Kautilya described governance and wealth management in terms of long cycles (yugas), emphasising that short-term sacrifices yield long-term prosperity. Modern Parallel: Long-term equity investment, Rupee Cost Averaging through SIPs, Compounding over decades 1.5.3 Comparative Table: Arthashastra vs. Modern Portfolio Management Arthashastra Principle Modern Equivalent Relevance in India Today Kosha Raksha (Treasury Protection) Capital Preservation / Risk Management SEBI’s IEPF, investor protection norms Bahubali Niti (Diversification of Power) Portfolio Diversification (MPT) Markowitz Efficient Frontier Vyavaharika Dharma (Ethical Trade) ESG Investing / Corporate Governance BRSR reporting, SEBI ESG framework Upaaya (Strategic Risk Assessment) Risk-Return Analysis / Scenario Planning VaR, Stress Testing, Scenario Analysis Danda (Enforcement / Stop-Loss) Stop-Loss Orders / Risk Limits F&O position limits, circuit breakers Kshetra Vibhaga (Resource Allocation) Asset Allocation Strategy Strategic Asset Allocation (SAA) Niti (Intelligence and Research) Fundamental & Technical Analysis SEBI research analyst regulations Yugas (Long-term Cycles) Long-term Investing / Compound Interest SIP discipline, long-term wealth creation Vyaya (Controlled Expenditure) Expense Ratio Minimisation / Cost Control Direct Mutual Fund plans, zero-commission Labha (Profit Maximisation with limits) Sharpe Ratio / Risk-Adjusted Returns Risk-adjusted performance benchmarking Arthashastra Principles vs. Modern Portfolio Management Source: Compiled by the author from Kautilya (300 BCE), Sharpe et al. (2020), and SEBI publications. Research Corner 1.1: Behavioural Finance and Arthashastra Recent research by Thaler and Sunstein (2008) and subsequent behavioural economists has demonstrated that investors routinely violate rational decision-making — they are loss-averse, overconfident, and herd-driven. Interestingly, Kautilya’s emphasis on intelligence, caution, and long-term thinking anticipates many of the de-biasing prescriptions of modern behavioural finance. Research Gap: A systematic study comparing Arthashastra’s wealth management doctrines with modern portfolio theory remains underexplored in Indian finance journals. This offers fertile ground for PhD research. Suggested Reading: - Rangarajan, L.N. (Ed.) (1992). Kautilya: The Arthashastra. Penguin Classics. - Thaler, R.H. & Sunstein, C.R. (2008). Nudge. Yale University Press. 1.6 Summary This unit laid the conceptual foundation for the entire textbook. The key takeaways are: Investment is a sacrifice of present certainty for future uncertain return — distinguished from speculation by its research-based, long-term orientation. Three characteristics drive every investment decision: Return, Risk, and Liquidity — and these three are in perpetual tension. Classification of investments into financial (equity, debt, derivatives), physical (real estate, gold), and hybrid (REITs, SGBs) helps match instruments to investor objectives. Four primary needs drive investment: wealth creation (compounding), inflation protection (real returns), retirement planning (EPF, PPF, NPS), and tax planning (Section 80C). Indian investment avenues span a wide spectrum — from ultra-safe G-Secs and PPF to market-linked equity and real estate — each with distinct risk, return, and tax profiles. Gold and silver occupy unique positions as alternative assets — gold as inflation hedge, silver as both precious metal and industrial commodity. Kautilya’s Arthashastra reveals that the principles of modern portfolio management — capital preservation, diversification, risk assessment, long-term thinking — have deep roots in Indian intellectual tradition. 1.7 Key Terms Investment — Commitment of funds today for uncertain future returns Speculation — Short-term, high-risk financial activity based on market movements Return — Income from investment (dividend, interest, capital gain) Risk — Probability that actual return will differ from expected return Liquidity — Ease of conversion of an asset to cash Safety — Protection of invested capital Government Securities (G-Secs) — Sovereign debt instruments issued by RBI Treasury Bills — Short-term government securities (up to 364 days) Sovereign Gold Bonds (SGBs) — RBI-issued gold-backed investment bonds PPF (Public Provident Fund) — 15-year government-backed savings scheme EPF (Employees’ Provident Fund) — Mandatory retirement scheme for salaried employees SCSS (Senior Citizens Savings Scheme) — Post office scheme for 60+ investors SSY (Sukanya Samriddhi Yojana) — Savings scheme for girl child education and marriage ULIP (Unit Linked Insurance Plan) — Market-linked insurance product by IRDAI REIT (Real Estate Investment Trust) — Listed trust owning income-generating real estate InvIT (Infrastructure Investment Trust) — Listed trust owning infrastructure assets Fixed Deposit (FD) — Bank deposit at fixed rate for fixed tenure Tax Efficiency — Minimisation of tax liability through investment planning Inflation Risk — Risk of purchasing power erosion Capital Gain — Profit from sale of investment above purchase price EEE Status — Exempt-Exempt-Exempt (contribution, accumulation, withdrawal) Section 80C — Income tax deduction of up to ₹1.5 lakh for specific investments Arthashastra — Ancient Indian treatise on statecraft and economics by Kautilya Kosha — Treasury in Kautilya’s framework; equivalent to capital preservation Compounding — Earning returns on previously earned returns SIP (Systematic Investment Plan) — Regular, fixed-amount investment in mutual funds Rupee Cost Averaging — Investment discipline of buying more units when prices are low DICGC — Deposit Insurance and Credit Guarantee Corporation (covers ₹5 lakh per depositor) RBI Retail Direct — Platform for retail investors to buy G-Secs directly from RBI NHB Residex — National Housing Bank’s residential price index for India 1.8 Review Questions 1.8.1 Very Short Answer Questions (2 marks each) Define investment. Chapter 2 Module II Learning Objectives After completing this unit, students will be able to: Explain the meaning, features, and types of shares, debentures, and other financial securities Distinguish between equity and preference shares, and among the types of each Analyse the structure, advantages, and risks of mutual fund investments Calculate Net Asset Value (NAV) and Expense Ratio with numerical illustrations Describe the SEBI categorisation of mutual fund schemes (2017–present) Evaluate Exchange Traded Funds (ETFs), REITs, and InvITs as investment instruments Assess the growth and future prospects of ETFs, REITs, and InvITs in the Indian market 2.1 2.1 Shares — Meaning and Features 2.1.1 2.1.1 What is a Share? A share is a unit of ownership in a company. When a company raises capital by issuing shares to the public, it distributes ownership of the company into small, equal units. Each unit is a share. The holder of a share is called a shareholder or stockholder and has a proportionate claim on the company’s assets and earnings. In India, shares are issued under the Companies Act, 2013 and are regulated by SEBI (Securities and Exchange Board of India). They are traded on stock exchanges — primarily the NSE (National Stock Exchange) and BSE (Bombay Stock Exchange). “A share is not just a piece of paper — it is a proportionate ownership of a living, breathing business enterprise.” — Benjamin Graham Share Capital Structure: \[	ext{Total Equity} = 	ext{Paid-up Capital} + 	ext{Reserves and Surplus}\] \[	ext{Book Value per Share} =  rac{	ext{Total Equity}}{	ext{Number of Outstanding Shares}}\] 2.1.2 2.1.2 Rights of Shareholders Shareholders enjoy several fundamental rights: Right to dividend — proportionate share in distributed profits Right to vote — one share, one vote (equity shareholders) on company matters Right to information — access to annual reports, quarterly results Right to transfer — free transfer of listed shares on stock exchanges Pre-emptive right (Right Issue) — first right to subscribe to new share issues Right to residual claims — claim on assets during liquidation (after creditors) Right to attend AGM — participate in Annual General Meetings 2.2 2.2 Equity Shares 2.2.1 2.2.1 Meaning and Features Equity shares (also called ordinary shares or common stock) represent the primary and permanent capital of a company. Equity shareholders are the true owners of the business and bear the highest risk. Key Features: - Variable dividend — declared by the Board, not fixed - Last in dividend priority — preference shareholders paid first - Residual claimants — in liquidation, equity holders receive what remains after all liabilities - Voting rights — full voting rights in all company matters - Unlimited upside — shares of companies like Infosys, TCS, and Reliance have created immense wealth - Perpetual existence — equity capital is permanent (no redemption) 2.2.2 2.2.2 Types of Equity Shares Table 2.1: Table 2.2: Table 2.1: Types of Equity Shares and Indian Examples Type Features Indian Example Ordinary / Common Shares Standard equity with full voting rights and dividend entitlement Reliance Industries, TCS, HDFC Bank Founder Shares / Promoter Shares Held by founders; may carry differential voting rights (DVR) Infosys founder shares (N.R. Narayana Murthy, Nilekani) Sweat Equity Shares Issued to directors/employees for know-how or IPR at discount ESOP-heavy tech startups: Zomato, Nykaa Employee Stock Option Plan (ESOP) Options to buy shares at predetermined price; reward tool Wipro, Infosys ESOP programmes Bonus Shares Issued free to existing shareholders from reserves TCS 1:1 bonus (2022), Asian Paints frequent bonuses Right Shares Offered to existing shareholders at discount before public issue Tata Motors Rights Issue (2021 — ₹15,000 Cr) Deferred Shares Dividend deferred until preference and equity holders are paid Historically used; rare in modern India Source: Compiled from Companies Act 2013, SEBI Regulations, and NSE/BSE corporate action data. Differential Voting Rights (DVR) Shares: SEBI amended regulations in 2019 to allow superior voting right shares (SR Shares) for promoters of technology companies during their IPO. This was triggered by concerns that founders of new-age companies (like Zomato, Paytm) would lose control post-IPO to institutional investors. The concept borrows from Google’s Class A / Class B share structure. 2.3 2.3 Preference Shares 2.3.1 2.3.1 Meaning and Features Preference shares occupy a middle position between equity shares and debentures. Preference shareholders have a preferential right over equity shareholders in: - Payment of dividends (fixed rate) - Repayment of capital in case of winding up However, preference shareholders generally do not have voting rights (unless dividends are in arrear for two consecutive years). \[	ext{Preference Dividend} = 	ext{Face Value} 	imes 	ext{Dividend Rate (\%)}\] 2.3.2 2.3.2 Types of Preference Shares Table 2.3: Table 2.4: Table 2.2: Types of Preference Shares Type Description Key_Feature Cumulative Unpaid dividends accumulate; must be paid before equity dividend Investor-protective; safe during loss years Non-Cumulative Lapsed dividends are not carried forward No backlog; company-friendly Participating Right to share in surplus profits after fixed dividend Upside participation in good years Non-Participating Only entitled to fixed dividend, no additional profit share Fixed income only Redeemable Redeemed by company after a specified period (max 20 yrs in India) Capital returned; SEBI regulated Irredeemable Not redeemed during company’s lifetime (banned in India post-1988) Permanent capital; phased out Convertible Can be converted into equity shares after specified period Equity upside with preference security Non-Convertible Cannot be converted into equity Pure fixed-income; no equity participation Source: Compiled from Companies Act, 2013 and SEBI Regulations. 2.4 2.4 Debentures 2.4.1 2.4.1 Meaning A debenture is a debt instrument issued by a company acknowledging its indebtedness. It represents a long-term loan to the company. Debenture holders are creditors, not owners, and have a legal right to receive: - Periodic interest (called coupon) - Repayment of principal at maturity In India, debentures issued to public must be listed on stock exchanges (NSE or BSE debt segment) and rated by credit rating agencies like CRISIL, ICRA, CARE, or India Ratings. 2.4.2 2.4.2 Types of Debentures Table 2.5: Table 2.6: Table 2.3: Types of Debentures Classification Basis Type Description On Security Secured Debentures Backed by charge on company’s assets; safer for investors Unsecured Debentures No asset backing; higher risk, higher yield On Convertibility Fully Convertible Debentures (FCDs) Entire amount converted to equity on specified date Partly Convertible Debentures (PCDs) Partial conversion to equity; balance redeemed Non-Convertible Debentures (NCDs) Not convertible; pure debt instrument; most common in India On Redemption Redeemable / Irredeemable Redeemable at par/premium on maturity; irredeemable rare On Coupon Fixed / Floating Rate Fixed coupon vs. linked to MIBOR/T-Bill rate Source: Compiled from Companies Act, 2013 and SEBI (LODR) Regulations, 2015. Indian NCD Market: Non-Convertible Debentures (NCDs) have become a popular retail investment avenue in India. Bajaj Finance, HDFC, Shriram Transport Finance, and Tata Capital have issued retail NCDs with coupon rates ranging from 7.5% to 10% p.a., offering returns superior to bank FDs with credit risk. 2.5 2.5 Mutual Funds 2.5.1 2.5.1 Meaning and Structure A mutual fund is a professionally managed collective investment vehicle that pools money from many investors and invests in a diversified portfolio of securities. In India, mutual funds are regulated by SEBI under the SEBI (Mutual Funds) Regulations, 1996 (amended multiple times through 2023). Figure 2.1: Three-tier Structure of Indian Mutual Funds Three-Tier Structure: 1. Sponsors — the parent company that establishes the mutual fund (e.g., HDFC Ltd. → HDFC AMC) 2. Trustees — independent board overseeing the AMC, protecting investors’ interests 3. AMC (Asset Management Company) — manages investments, employs fund managers Key Industry Statistics: Figure 2.1: Figure 2.2: Indian Mutual Fund Industry AUM Growth (2016–2026) Source: Compiled from RBI, SEBI, NSE, BSE, AMFI and Government Reports (Latest Available Data). 2.5.2 2.5.2 Advantages of Mutual Funds Professional Management — Experienced fund managers conduct research and manage portfolios Diversification — Even ₹500 SIP invests in 50–100 stocks Affordability — Start SIP with as low as ₹100/month Liquidity — Open-end funds redeemable any business day at NAV Transparency — Daily NAV disclosure; monthly portfolio disclosure Regulatory Protection — SEBI regulations protect investors; AMFI code of conduct Tax Efficiency — ELSS funds offer Section 80C benefits; long-term capital gains taxed at 10% (>₹1 lakh) Flexibility — SIP, SWP, STP options; switch between funds Economies of Scale — Lower transaction costs due to bulk purchases Variety — 40+ AMCs, 1,500+ schemes across all risk profiles 2.5.3 2.5.3 Risks in Mutual Fund Investment Risk Type Description Affected Funds Market Risk Portfolio value falls with market Equity funds Credit Risk Issuer defaults on bond payment Debt funds Interest Rate Risk Bond prices fall when rates rise Debt funds Liquidity Risk Fund unable to sell assets Close-end funds Concentration Risk Overdependence on few stocks/sectors Thematic funds Currency Risk Loss from foreign exchange movement International funds Manager Risk Fund manager’s incorrect decisions All actively managed funds Inflation Risk Returns don’t beat inflation Conservative funds Source: Compiled from RBI, SEBI, NSE, BSE, AMFI and Government Reports (Latest Available Data). 2.6 2.6 Net Asset Value (NAV) 2.6.1 2.6.1 Meaning of NAV The Net Asset Value (NAV) is the per-unit market value of a mutual fund scheme. It represents the price at which investors can buy (subscription) or sell (redemption) units of the fund. \[ oxed{NAV =  rac{	ext{Total Assets} - 	ext{Total Liabilities}}{	ext{Number of Units Outstanding}}}\] Components: - Total Assets = Market value of securities + Cash and equivalents + Receivables + Accrued income - Total Liabilities = Payables + Accrued expenses + Management fee payable 2.6.2 2.6.2 NAV Calculation — Worked Example Example 2.1: Calculate the NAV of “Axis Bluechip Fund” given the following data as on March 31, 2024: Component Amount (₹ Crore) Equity portfolio market value 8,450 Liquid funds / Cash 320 Dividend receivable 48 Other receivables 22 Management fee payable 15 Other liabilities 8 Number of units 250 crore Solution: \[	ext{Total Assets} = 8,450 + 320 + 48 + 22 = ₹8,840 	ext{ Cr}\] \[	ext{Total Liabilities} = 15 + 8 = ₹23 	ext{ Cr}\] \[	ext{Net Assets} = 8,840 - 23 = ₹8,817 	ext{ Cr}\] \[NAV =  rac{8,817 	ext{ Cr}}{250 	ext{ Cr units}} = ₹35.27 	ext{ per unit}\] # NAV Calculation total_assets <- 8450 + 320 + 48 + 22 # in Crore total_liabilities <- 15 + 8 net_assets <- total_assets - total_liabilities units_outstanding <- 250 # Crore units NAV <- net_assets / units_outstanding cat("Total Assets: ₹", total_assets, "Crore
") ## Total Assets: ₹ 8840 Crore cat("Total Liabilities: ₹", total_liabilities, "Crore
") ## Total Liabilities: ₹ 23 Crore cat("Net Assets: ₹", net_assets, "Crore
") ## Net Assets: ₹ 8817 Crore cat("NAV per unit: ₹", round(NAV, 2), "
") ## NAV per unit: ₹ 35.27 2.6.3 2.6.3 NAV and Returns Absolute Return: \[	ext{Absolute Return (\%)} =  rac{NAV_{current} - NAV_{purchase}}{NAV_{purchase}} 	imes 100\] CAGR (for periods > 1 year): \[CAGR = \left( rac{NAV_{current}}{NAV_{purchase}}ight)^{ rac{1}{n}} - 1\] Example 2.2: An investor purchased units of HDFC Flexi Cap Fund at NAV ₹18.50 on April 1, 2016. The NAV on March 31, 2024 was ₹52.80. Calculate absolute return and CAGR. nav_purchase <- 18.50 nav_current <- 52.80 years_held <- 8 absolute_return <- ((nav_current - nav_purchase) / nav_purchase) * 100 cagr <- (nav_current / nav_purchase)^(1/years_held) - 1 cat("Absolute Return:", round(absolute_return, 2), "%
") ## Absolute Return: 185.41 % cat("CAGR:", round(cagr * 100, 2), "% per annum
") ## CAGR: 14.01 % per annum 2.7 2.7 Expense Ratio 2.7.1 2.7.1 Meaning and Importance The Expense Ratio (ER) is the annual cost charged by a mutual fund to manage an investor’s money, expressed as a percentage of the fund’s daily average AUM. It covers: - Fund management fee (largest component) - Trustee fee - Registrar and transfer agent fee - Marketing and distribution expenses (only in Regular Plans) - Audit, legal, and custodian fees \[ oxed{	ext{Expense Ratio (\%)} =  rac{	ext{Total Annual Fund Expenses}}{	ext{Average Daily AUM}} 	imes 100}\] SEBI Expense Ratio Limits (as of 2024): Table 2.7: Table 2.8: Table 2.4: SEBI Maximum TER Limits for Mutual Funds AUM Slab Equity TER Max Debt TER Max Index/ETF Max First ₹500 Cr 2.25% 2.00% 1.00% Next ₹250 Cr 2.00% 1.75% 1.00% Next ₹1,250 Cr 1.75% 1.50% 1.00% Next ₹3,000 Cr 1.60% 1.35% 1.00% Next ₹5,000 Cr 1.50% 1.25% 1.00% Next ₹40,000 Cr Reduce by 0.05% per ₹5,000 Cr Reduce by 0.05% 1.00% Balance above ₹50,000 Cr 1.05% 0.80% 1.00% Source: Compiled from RBI, SEBI, NSE, BSE, AMFI and Government Reports (Latest Available Data). 2.7.2 2.7.2 Expense Ratio Calculation — Worked Example Example 2.3: A mutual fund has daily average AUM of ₹12,000 Crore. Annual expenses: Management fee ₹108 Cr, Distribution ₹36 Cr, Trustee fee ₹6 Cr, Others ₹10 Cr. Calculate Total Expense Ratio. aum <- 12000 # Crore management_fee <- 108 distribution <- 36 trustee <- 6 others <- 10 total_expenses <- management_fee + distribution + trustee + others TER <- (total_expenses / aum) * 100 cat("Total Annual Expenses: ₹", total_expenses, "Crore
") ## Total Annual Expenses: ₹ 160 Crore cat("Average AUM: ₹", aum, "Crore
") ## Average AUM: ₹ 12000 Crore cat("Total Expense Ratio (TER):", round(TER, 3), "%
") ## Total Expense Ratio (TER): 1.333 % cat("Impact on ₹1 Lakh invested:", round(100000 * TER/100), "per annum
") ## Impact on ₹1 Lakh invested: 1333 per annum Direct vs. Regular Plans: SEBI mandated Direct Plans in 2013 — these exclude distributor commissions and hence have lower expense ratios by 0.5%–1.2%. Over 20 years, this difference compounds significantly. monthly_sip <- 10000 months <- 240 gross_return <- 0.12 / 12 # Monthly # Regular plan: 12% gross, 1.5% TER → ~10.5% net return regular_return <- (0.12 - 0.015) / 12 direct_return <- (0.12 - 0.005) / 12 # Direct: 0.5% TER fv_regular <- monthly_sip * (((1 + regular_return)^months - 1) / regular_return) fv_direct <- monthly_sip * (((1 + direct_return)^months - 1) / direct_return) cat("20-Year SIP in Regular Plan: ₹", format(round(fv_regular), big.mark = ","), "
") ## 20-Year SIP in Regular Plan: ₹ 8,105,049 cat("20-Year SIP in Direct Plan: ₹", format(round(fv_direct), big.mark = ","), "
") ## 20-Year SIP in Direct Plan: ₹ 9,251,011 cat("Extra wealth from Direct Plan: ₹", format(round(fv_direct - fv_regular), big.mark = ","), "
") ## Extra wealth from Direct Plan: ₹ 1,145,962 # Plot comparison year_seq <- 1:20 corpus_regular <- sapply(year_seq, function(y) { m <- y * 12 monthly_sip * (((1 + regular_return)^m - 1) / regular_return) }) corpus_direct <- sapply(year_seq, function(y) { m <- y * 12 monthly_sip * (((1 + direct_return)^m - 1) / direct_return) }) plot_df <- data.frame( Year = rep(year_seq, 2), Corpus = c(corpus_regular, corpus_direct) / 100000, Plan = rep(c("Regular Plan (TER 1.5%)", "Direct Plan (TER 0.5%)"), each = 20) ) ggplot(plot_df, aes(x = Year, y = Corpus, color = Plan)) + geom_line(size = 1.4) + geom_point(size = 2) + scale_color_manual(values = c("Regular Plan (TER 1.5%)" = "#c0392b", "Direct Plan (TER 0.5%)" = "#27ae60")) + labs(title = "Direct vs. Regular Plan: Impact of Expense Ratio", subtitle = "SIP ₹10,000/Month, 20 Years, 12% Gross Return", x = "Year", y = "Corpus (₹ Lakhs)", color = "") + theme_minimal(base_size = 13) + theme(legend.position = "bottom") Figure 2.2: Figure 2.3: Direct Plan vs. Regular Plan Wealth Creation (20-Year SIP ₹10,000/Month) Source: Compiled from RBI, SEBI, NSE, BSE, AMFI and Government Reports (Latest Available Data). 2.8 2.8 Types of Mutual Funds 2.8.1 2.8.1 SEBI Categorisation of Mutual Fund Schemes (2017) SEBI issued a landmark circular on mutual fund categorisation and rationalisation in October 2017 (effective June 2018), mandating that each AMC can offer only one scheme per category. This brought standardisation and comparability to India’s mutual fund landscape. Figure 2.4: SEBI Categorisation of Mutual Fund Schemes (2017) 2.8.2 2.8.2 Major Categories — Detailed Analysis A. Large Cap Funds - Must invest minimum 80% in top 100 companies by market capitalisation (as defined by AMFI) - Lower risk than mid/small cap; benchmark: Nifty 50 / Sensex - Suitable for conservative equity investors - Examples: Axis Bluechip Fund, Mirae Asset Large Cap Fund, ICICI Prudential Bluechip Fund B. Mid Cap Funds - Must invest minimum 65% in 101st–250th companies by market cap - Higher growth potential; higher volatility - Examples: HDFC Mid-Cap Opportunities Fund, Kotak Emerging Equity Fund C. Small Cap Funds - Minimum 65% in 251st company onwards by market cap - Highest growth potential and highest risk among equity categories - Examples: Nippon India Small Cap Fund, SBI Small Cap Fund D. ELSS (Equity Linked Savings Scheme) - Minimum 80% in equity - 3-year lock-in (shortest among 80C investments) - Section 80C benefit up to ₹1.5 lakh - Examples: Axis Long Term Equity, Mirae Asset Tax Saver Fund E. Liquid Funds - Invest in money market instruments with maturity up to 91 days - Very low risk; returns slightly above savings account (5.5–6.5% p.a.) - Suitable for emergency fund management F. Gilt Funds - Invest 80% minimum in government securities - Zero credit risk; high interest rate risk - Suitable when interest rates are expected to fall Figure 2.3: Figure 2.5: Mutual Fund Category Risk-Return Matrix (2016–2026) Source: Compiled from RBI, SEBI, NSE, BSE, AMFI and Government Reports (Latest Available Data). SIP Growth in India (2016–2026): Figure 2.4: Figure 2.6: SIP Monthly Contribution and Folios (2016–2026) Source: Compiled from RBI, SEBI, NSE, BSE, AMFI and Government Reports (Latest Available Data). 2.9 2.9 Exchange Traded Funds (ETFs) 2.9.1 2.9.1 Meaning and Structure An Exchange Traded Fund (ETF) is a mutual fund that is listed and traded on stock exchanges like shares, while tracking a specific index, commodity, or basket of assets. ETFs combine the diversification benefits of mutual funds with the trading flexibility of stocks. Key Features of ETFs: - Passively managed (track an index) - Lower expense ratio than actively managed funds (0.05%–0.50%) - Real-time pricing (unlike NAV-based mutual funds) - Can be bought/sold anytime during market hours - Minimum investment = 1 unit (at market price) - No exit load in most cases - Require demat account (unlike regular mutual funds) ETF vs. Index Fund: Feature ETF Index Fund Trading Real-time on exchange End-of-day NAV Demat Required Not required Minimum Amount 1 unit ₹100–₹500 SIP Expense Ratio 0.05%–0.25% 0.15%–0.50% Tracking Error Slightly higher Lower Liquidity Higher (intraday) T+3 redemption ETF Growth in India: Figure 2.5: Figure 2.7: ETF AUM Growth in India (2016–2026) Source: Compiled from RBI, SEBI, NSE, BSE, AMFI and Government Reports (Latest Available Data). Special Feature: EPFO and ETFs A landmark development in Indian capital markets was the EPFO’s decision in 2015 to invest 5% (later increased to 15%) of incremental EPF corpus in equity ETFs tracking Nifty 50 and Sensex. This makes EPFO one of the largest domestic institutional investors through the ETF route, protecting subscribers’ retirement savings through passive equity exposure. 2.10 2.10 Real Estate Investment Trusts (REITs) 2.10.1 2.10.1 Meaning and Structure A Real Estate Investment Trust (REIT) is a listed entity that owns, operates, or finances income-generating real estate assets. REITs allow retail investors to invest in large commercial properties (office parks, malls, hospitals) with a minimum of ₹10,000–15,000. SEBI introduced REIT regulations in 2014, and the first Indian REIT — Embassy Office Parks REIT — was listed on NSE in April 2019. SEBI introduced InvIT regulations in 2014. India’s first InvIT — IRB InvIT Fund — listed in May 2017. Research Corner 2.1: The Future of Passive Investing in India The global shift from active to passive investing is accelerating in India. Key research observations: SPIVA India Scorecard (S&P, 2024) shows that over 10 years, over 80% of large-cap active funds underperformed the Nifty 50 TRI (Total Return Index). EPFO’s ETF mandate has democratised passive investing, with ₹2.5+ lakh crore invested in equity ETFs by EPFO as of 2024. Zero-commission direct plans and growing financial literacy are driving retail investors toward direct index ETFs. Research Gap: The tracking error of Indian ETFs — especially for mid-cap and small-cap indices — remains significantly higher than developed market ETFs. Research into optimal ETF creation/redemption mechanisms for Indian markets is needed. 2.12 2.12 Summary Shares are units of company ownership. Equity shareholders are residual claimants with voting rights; preference shareholders have priority in dividend and capital repayment. Debentures are debt instruments — NCDs, FCDs, and PCDs represent the primary classifications used in Indian markets. CRISIL, ICRA, and CARE ratings guide investor decisions. Mutual funds are the most accessible financial investment vehicle for retail investors — professionally managed, diversified, and SEBI-regulated. India’s MF industry crossed ₹67 lakh crore AUM in 2026. NAV is the per-unit value of a mutual fund, calculated daily. Total returns depend on NAV appreciation plus dividend distributions. Expense Ratio directly erodes investor returns. Direct plans save 0.5%–1.2% annually, compounding to significant wealth over 15–20 years. SEBI’s 2017 categorisation brought standardisation — one scheme per category per AMC — enabling investors to make meaningful comparisons. ETFs track indices passively at very low cost and can be traded intraday. EPFO’s ETF mandate is a landmark development for Indian capital markets. REITs enable retail investors to invest in commercial real estate with ₹10,000–15,000, earning distribution yields of 6–8% p.a. Four REITs are listed in India (2024). InvITs invest in operating infrastructure projects (roads, power lines, gas pipelines), offering yields of 10–12% p.a. with government-backed revenue streams. 2.13 Key Terms Share — Unit of ownership in a company Equity Share — Ordinary share with full ownership rights and residual claims Preference Share — Share with priority dividend rights over equity Cumulative Preference Share — Unpaid dividends accumulate for future payment Convertible Preference Share — Convertible into equity shares at specified date Debenture — Long-term debt instrument issued by companies NCD (Non-Convertible Debenture) — Debenture that cannot be converted to equity ESOP — Employee Stock Option Plan; Chapter 6 Trading View Practicals 6.1 Program 1: SMA Trend Identification & Dynamic Support/Resistance Objective Identify trend direction and find dynamic support/resistance levels using the Simple Moving Average (SMA) — applied here to Indian stock market (NSE/BSE) charts on TradingView. Steps Step 1: Go to TradingView and Open the Chart Section Open your browser and navigate to tradingview.com. Sign in to your account (or create one if you haven’t already — it’s free). From the top navigation bar, click on “Products” or directly click “Chart” to launch the chart workspace. Step 2: Select an NSE Stock and Set the Timeframe Click on the symbol search bar at the top-left of the chart (it may currently show a default ticker). Type the stock name — for example, RELIANCE. From the dropdown results, make sure to select the listing tagged NSE (National Stock Exchange of India), since TradingView also lists the same company on other exchanges. It should appear as NSE:RELIANCE. Other popular NSE examples you can try: NSE:TCS, NSE:HDFCBANK, NSE:INFY, NSE:ICICIBANK, NSE:SBIN. Once selected, the candlestick chart for Reliance Industries will load, denominated in ₹ (INR). Now set the timeframe for long-term investing analysis. Click on the timeframe selector in the top toolbar (it usually shows “1D” by default). Select 1W (1 Week) from the dropdown, or type 1W directly into the timeframe box and press Enter. The chart will now display weekly candles — each candle represents one full week of trading on NSE, which smooths out daily noise and is ideal for long-term trend analysis. Why weekly timeframe for Indian long-term investing? NSE has frequent intraday volatility, especially around results season, RBI policy announcements, and budget days. A weekly chart filters this noise and reveals the broader trend more reliably for long-term positioning. Step 3: Open the Indicators Panel Look at the top toolbar of the chart and click on “Indicators” (it may show as “Indicators” with an fx or function-style icon, sometimes abbreviated visually). A panel titled “Indicators & Strategies” will pop up, with a search bar at the top. In the search box, type “Simple Moving Average”. You’ll see one or more matching results in the list (TradingView sometimes shows it twice — once as a standalone built-in indicator, and once under community scripts). Use the built-in “Moving Average Simple” — it’s the official one maintained by TradingView and is sufficient for this exercise. Step 4: Add the SMA and Set the Length to 50 Click on “Moving Average Simple” from the search results. It will be added to your chart instantly with a default length (usually 9). To customize it, click the gear/settings icon next to the SMA name in the legend at the top-left of the chart (it appears once the indicator is added), or double-click directly on the SMA line on the chart. In the settings window that opens, find the “Length” field under the Inputs tab. Clear the existing value and type 50. Click OK to apply. The chart will now redraw with a 50-period SMA line overlaid on the weekly candles. Since you’re on the weekly timeframe, this represents the 50-week SMA — roughly the average closing price over the last ~50 weeks (about 11–12 months), which is a popular long-term trend filter among Indian long-term investors and positional traders. Step 5: Interpret the SMA on the Chart Once the SMA 50 is plotted, observe where the current price sits relative to the line: Price Above SMA 50 → Uptrend: The stock is trading above its 50-week average, suggesting sustained buying interest and a bullish long-term structure. Many Indian portfolio managers use this as a basic “trend filter” before considering fresh long-term buys. Price Below SMA 50 → Downtrend: The stock is trading below its 50-week average, signaling weakness or a corrective/bearish phase. This is often used as a caution flag to avoid fresh long-term entries or to review existing holdings. SMA as Dynamic Support or Resistance: Unlike a fixed horizontal support/resistance line, the SMA moves with price over time. In an uptrend, the SMA 50 frequently acts as a “floor” where price pulls back to and bounces from. In a downtrend, it often acts as a “ceiling” that price rallies up to and gets rejected from. Trading Rules Signal Condition Action  BUY Price approaches the 50 SMA from above and bounces off it (forms a reversal candle near the line) Consider entering or adding to a long-term position  SELL Price approaches the 50 SMA from below and gets rejected (fails to close above it) Consider booking profits or avoiding fresh entry Indian market context note: Apply this technique to liquid, fundamentally sound NSE large-cap or Nifty 50 stocks for long-term investing decisions. For small-cap or low-liquidity stocks, SMA bounces can be less reliable due to erratic price action and lower trading volumes. Always combine this technical signal with basic fundamental checks (sector outlook, quarterly results, promoter holding trends) before making investment decisions — this manual covers a technical tool, not complete investment advice. Practice Exercise Open NSE:TCS on the weekly timeframe and add a 50 SMA. Identify whether the stock is currently in an uptrend or downtrend based on price position relative to the SMA. Scroll back through the last 2–3 years of price history and mark at least two instances where price bounced off the 50 SMA, and one instance where it was rejected by the SMA. Repeat the exercise for NSE:HDFCBANK and compare the trend structure between the two stocks. Key Takeaways The 50 SMA on a weekly chart is a simple, widely-used trend filter for long-term investing decisions on NSE-listed stocks. Price position relative to the SMA (above vs. below) gives a quick read on the prevailing trend. The SMA also acts as a dynamic support/resistance zone, offering potential entry (bounce) and exit (rejection) cues. Always combine this with broader market context (Nifty 50 trend, sector performance) before acting on signals from a single stock chart. 6.2 Program 2: EMA Crossover Breakout Strategy Objective To identify breakout and momentum opportunities using the Exponential Moving Average (EMA) crossover technique — applied to Indian stock market (NSE/BSE) charts on TradingView. Why EMA instead of SMA here? The Exponential Moving Average (EMA) gives more weight to recent price action compared to the SMA, making it react faster to new momentum. This makes it well-suited for spotting breakouts early — useful for Indian long-term investors who want to catch the start of a fresh up-move in stocks like Nifty 50 constituents. Steps Step 1: Open the Indicators Panel With your NSE stock chart open, click on “Indicators” in the top toolbar of the chart (shown with an fx-style icon, labeled “Indicators”). The “Indicators & Strategies” search panel will open. Step 2: Search for “Moving Average Exponential” In the search bar at the top of the panel, type “Moving Average Exponential”. From the results list, select the built-in “Moving Average Exponential” indicator (maintained directly by TradingView, distinct from any community/script versions). Step 3: Add the First EMA — Set Length to 20, Color Green Once selected, the EMA will be added to your chart with a default length (usually 9). Click the gear/settings icon next to the EMA’s name in the chart legend (top-left of the chart) to open its settings. Under the Inputs tab, change the Length field to 20. Switch to the Style tab and change the line color to Green for easy visual identification. Click OK to apply. Your chart now shows a 20-period EMA in green — on the weekly timeframe, this is your 20-week EMA, representing short-to-medium-term momentum. Step 4: Duplicate the EMA to Add a Second One In the chart legend (top-left), right-click on the EMA 20 entry — or click the three-dot menu (⋮) next to it. From the dropdown menu, select “Duplicate”. This creates an exact copy of the EMA 20 indicator, stacked on the same chart, which you’ll now modify into a second, longer-period EMA. Step 5: Configure the Second EMA — Set Length to 50, Color Red Click the gear/settings icon next to the newly duplicated EMA in the legend. Under the Inputs tab, change the Length from 20 to 50. Switch to the Style tab and change the line color to Red. Click OK to apply. Your chart now displays two EMAs: EMA 20 (Green) — faster, tracks short-term momentum EMA 50 (Red) — slower, tracks the broader trend Step 6: Observe the EMA Crossovers on the Chart With both EMAs plotted, watch for the points where the green (20) line crosses the red (50) line: These crossover points are the core signal of this strategy and will appear at multiple points across the stock’s price history on NSE charts — particularly visible during strong trending phases like post-results rallies or sectoral rotations. Interpretation Crossover Type Visual Cue Meaning  20 EMA crosses above 50 EMA Green line moves above red line Bullish Breakout — short-term momentum is turning positive relative to the broader trend  20 EMA crosses below 50 EMA Green line moves below red line Bearish Breakout — short-term momentum is turning negative relative to the broader trend Indian market context note: This is sometimes informally compared to a faster version of the “Golden Cross / Death Cross” concept widely followed in Indian financial media (where 50-day/200-day crossovers are reported for the Nifty 50 and Sensex). The 20/50 EMA crossover on a weekly chart is a more sensitive, earlier-warning version suited for individual NSE stocks rather than just benchmark indices. Trading Rule  BUY: Enter when there is a bullish crossover (20 EMA crosses above 50 EMA) AND the current price is trading above both EMAs. This double confirmation — crossover plus price position — helps filter out weak or false breakout signals that can occur in choppy, sideways NSE stock action.  SELL / AVOID: Treat a bearish crossover (20 EMA crosses below 50 EMA) with price below both EMAs as a signal to avoid fresh entries or to review existing long positions in that stock. Practice Exercise Open NSE:INFY on the Weekly timeframe and apply both the 20 EMA (green) and 50 EMA (red) as described above. Scroll through the last 2–3 years of price history and identify at least two bullish crossovers and one bearish crossover. For each bullish crossover found, check whether price was trading above both EMAs at that point — this confirms whether it met the full BUY rule. Repeat with NSE:SBIN and compare how frequently crossovers occur in a banking stock versus an IT stock — note any difference in reliability or noise. Key Takeaways The EMA reacts faster to price than the SMA, making the 20/50 EMA crossover a useful tool for spotting early breakout momentum in NSE stocks. A crossover alone is not sufficient — always confirm with price position relative to both EMAs before acting, to avoid false signals in sideways markets. This strategy works best on liquid, fundamentally strong stocks and during clearly trending market phases; it can generate frequent false signals during range-bound or highly volatile periods, which are common around Indian budget announcements, RBI policy days, and global macro events. Combine EMA crossover signals with the broader trend context from Program 1 (SMA 50) for a more complete long-term investing view. 6.3 Program 3: VWAP Intraday Trend Strategy Objective To trade intraday using the Volume-Weighted Average Price (VWAP) on Indian stock market (NSE) charts on TradingView. Important context shift: Unlike Programs 1 and 2, which used weekly charts for long-term trend analysis, this strategy is intraday — meaning all trading activity happens and is squared off within the same trading session. On NSE, the cash market trading session runs from 9:15 AM to 3:30 PM IST. If you place an intraday order (commonly labeled MIS — Margin Intraday Square-off — with most Indian brokers like Zerodha, Upstox, or Groww), it will be automatically closed out by the broker shortly before market close if you haven’t exited manually, typically around 3:15–3:20 PM IST. VWAP resets fresh at the start of every new trading day and is not meant to be analyzed across multiple days like the SMA/EMA in earlier programs. Steps Step 1: Set the Timeframe to 5 Minutes With your NSE stock chart open, click on the timeframe selector in the top toolbar (it may currently show “1D”, “1W”, or whatever was last used). Select 5m (5 Minutes) from the dropdown, or type 5 directly into the timeframe box and press Enter. The chart will now display 5-minute candles — each candle represents 5 minutes of trading activity within the NSE session. This shorter timeframe is essential for intraday strategies, as it captures the granular price-volume action needed for VWAP analysis. Step 2: Open the Indicators Panel Click on “Indicators” in the top toolbar (shown with the fx-style icon). The “Indicators & Strategies” search panel will open with a search bar at the top. Step 3: Search and Add “VWAP” In the search box, type VWAP. From the results, select the built-in “VWAP” indicator (officially maintained by TradingView). Click on it once to add it directly to your chart — no length or period setting is required, since VWAP is a session-based (intraday-anchored) indicator by default and recalculates automatically from the start of each trading session. Step 4: VWAP Added to the Chart The VWAP line will now appear overlaid on your 5-minute candlestick chart. Notice that the VWAP line starts fresh each trading day from the 9:15 AM opening candle and builds up through the session as more price and volume data accumulates — it does not carry over from the previous day. As the day progresses, observe how price weaves above and below this line, and how the line itself smooths out compared to the choppier candle action. Interpretation Observation Visual Cue Meaning  Price Above VWAP Candles trading above the VWAP line Indicates institutional buying — large players (FIIs, DIIs, mutual funds) are likely accumulating at or above this average price, a bullish intraday signal  Price Below VWAP Candles trading below the VWAP line Indicates selling pressure — large sell orders are weighing on price below the session average, a bearish intraday signal ⚫ VWAP as Intraday Support/Resistance Price repeatedly touching and reacting to the VWAP line The VWAP line itself often behaves like a dynamic support (in an uptrending session) or resistance (in a downtrending session) level through the day Why VWAP matters in the Indian intraday context: Institutional desks, mutual funds, and FII/DII trading desks frequently use VWAP as a benchmark to evaluate whether their own large block orders on NSE are executing at favorable prices. Because of this, VWAP tends to act as a genuine “fair value” reference point during the day — retail intraday traders watch it for the same reason, making it a self-reinforcing level. Trading Rule  BUY: Enter when price pulls back to the VWAP line and reverses upward — i.e., the stock dips down to touch or approach VWAP during an otherwise uptrending session, forms a reversal candle (such as a bullish engulfing or hammer-like candle), and resumes its move higher. This suggests the pullback found support at the institutional average price level.  AVOID / EXIT: If price breaks decisively below VWAP and stays below it, treat this as a loss of intraday bullish momentum — consider exiting existing long positions rather than holding through the session. A Note on Risk Management for Indian Intraday Trading Since this is an intraday (MIS) strategy, always set a stop-loss below recent swing lows or a fixed percentage of capital risk per trade — NSE intraday volatility (especially around results, RBI announcements, or global cues like US Fed decisions) can move prices sharply within minutes. Be mindful of margin and leverage rules set by SEBI and your broker for intraday (MIS) trades — leverage amounts vary by stock and broker, and positions must be squared off within the same session or they may be auto-closed by your broker’s risk management system (RMS), sometimes at unfavorable prices. Avoid taking fresh intraday VWAP trades in the first 5–10 minutes after market open (9:15–9:25 AM), as this period often sees erratic price discovery before VWAP becomes a meaningful reference. Practice Exercise Open NSE:HDFCBANK on the 5-minute timeframe during a live trading session (9:15 AM – 3:30 PM IST) and add the VWAP indicator. Track the stock for at least one hour and note every instance where price touches the VWAP line. Identify whether each touch resulted in a bounce (continuation of trend) or a breakdown (trend reversal). Repeat this observation on a different day with NSE:TCS and compare how VWAP behaves on a relatively calmer IT stock versus a more news-sensitive banking stock. Key Takeaways VWAP is a session-based intraday indicator that resets daily and reflects the true volume-weighted average price paid by all market participants during that NSE trading session. Price position relative to VWAP gives a quick read on whether institutional money is net buying or net selling during the day. VWAP pullback-and-reversal setups offer disciplined intraday entry points, but always pair them with a stop-loss given the leveraged nature of intraday trading. This strategy is best suited to liquid, high-volume NSE large-cap stocks; thinly traded stocks can produce unreliable VWAP signals due to inconsistent volume. 6.4 Program 3B: Anchored VWAP for Long-Term Investing Objective To adapt the Volume-Weighted Average Price concept for long-term investing using Anchored VWAP (AVWAP) — a version of VWAP that does not reset daily, making it usable across weeks, months, and years on NSE stock charts. Program 3 covered the standard session VWAP, which resets every trading day and is built specifically for intraday use — it has no meaningful interpretation on weekly or monthly charts. However, the underlying idea behind VWAP (the average price weighted by how much volume traded at each price level) is genuinely useful for long-term investors too — it just needs a different version of the tool. Anchored VWAP (AVWAP) solves this. Instead of recalculating from the start of each day, you manually “anchor” it to a specific date you choose — such as a 52-week low, a major results announcement, or the start of a financial year — and it then calculates the volume-weighted average price continuously from that anchor point forward, for as long as you want to track it (weeks, months, or years). Key distinction: Session VWAP answers “did today’s buyers get a fair price today?” Anchored VWAP answers “what is the average price paid by everyone who has bought this stock since this important date?” The second question is far more relevant to long-term investing. Steps Step 1: Set the Timeframe With your NSE stock chart open, click on the timeframe selector in the top toolbar. Select 1D (Daily) for a medium-term view, or 1W (Weekly) for a more zoomed-out long-term view — both work well with Anchored VWAP since, unlike session VWAP, it doesn’t depend on the candle interval. For this exercise, use the Daily (1D) timeframe, as it gives you finer control over picking an exact anchor date in the next step. Step 2: Open the Indicators Panel Click on “Indicators” in the top toolbar (the fx-style icon). The “Indicators & Strategies” search panel will open. Step 3: Search and Add “Anchored VWAP” In the search box, type Anchored VWAP. From the results, select the built-in “Anchored VWAP” indicator (maintained directly by TradingView — distinct from any community scripts with similar names). Click on it once. TradingView will typically place the anchor automatically at the leftmost visible candle on your current chart view as a starting default. Step 4: Choose and Set Your Anchor Point This is the most important step — the anchor date you pick determines what the AVWAP line represents. Common long-term investing anchor points for Indian stocks include: 52-week low — to see the average price paid by everyone who has bought since the stock’s worst point in the last year (useful for spotting value re-accumulation). Start of the financial year (April 1st) or calendar year (January 1st) — to track average cost basis for the current investing year, aligned with how Indian mutual funds and DIIs often report performance. Day after a major quarterly results announcement — to see how the market has averaged price in reaction to that specific result. A major budget day or RBI policy announcement — to study sustained reactions to macro events affecting Indian markets. To set this: Click and drag the anchor marker directly on the chart to your chosen date, or Click the gear/settings icon next to “Anchored VWAP” in the chart legend, go to the Inputs tab, and manually enter the specific anchor date. Click OK to apply. The AVWAP line will now begin plotting from your selected anchor date and continue forward to the present day. Step 5: Anchored VWAP Added to the Chart You will now see a single VWAP line starting from your chosen anchor point and extending across all subsequent daily (or weekly) candles, smoothing as more time passes. Unlike session VWAP, this line does not reset — it keeps calculating cumulatively from the anchor date until you change or remove it. You can add multiple Anchored VWAPs with different anchor dates on the same chart (e.g., one anchored to the 52-week low and another anchored to April 1st) by repeating Steps 3–4 and assigning each a different color for clarity. Interpretation Observation Visual Cue Meaning  Price Above Anchored VWAP Candles trading above the AVWAP line The average buyer since the anchor date is in profit — reflects sustained accumulation and bullish sentiment since that event  Price Below Anchored VWAP Candles trading below the AVWAP line The average buyer since the anchor date is at a loss — reflects distribution or weak sentiment since that event ⚫ AVWAP as Long-Term Support/Resistance Price repeatedly returning to and reacting off the AVWAP line The anchored level often acts as a “memory” price — a zone where long-term holders defend their average cost, creating real support or resistance Indian market context: Anchored VWAP is particularly useful around results season (Q1–Q4 earnings on NSE) and around Union Budget day, since Indian institutional investors (FIIs/DIIs) often re-anchor their own cost-basis tracking to these dates. Watching where price sits relative to a results-day or budget-day AVWAP gives a sense of whether the broader market has digested that event positively or negatively over the following weeks and months. Trading Rule  BUY: Consider entering or adding to a long-term position when price pulls back to a meaningful Anchored VWAP (e.g., one anchored to the 52-week low or to the start of the financial year) and shows signs of holding/reversing upward — similar logic to the SMA 50 bounce rule in Program 1, but using a volume-weighted reference instead of a simple price average.  CAUTION: If price decisively breaks below a long-standing Anchored VWAP that has acted as support for multiple weeks or months, treat this as a signal that the long-term holder base may be turning net negative — review the position or fundamental thesis before adding further. Combining With Earlier Programs This chapter works well alongside the tools from Programs 1 and 2: Use SMA 50 (Program 1) for a general trend filter. Use EMA 20/50 crossover (Program 2) for momentum confirmation. Use Anchored VWAP (this chapter) anchored to a meaningful event (52-week low, financial year start, major results date) as a volume-aware reference for “fair value since that event” — adding a layer that pure moving averages don’t capture, since AVWAP factors in how much was traded at each price, not just the price itself. Practice Exercise Open NSE:TCS on the Daily timeframe and add an Anchored VWAP anchored to its most recent 52-week low. Note whether the current price is above or below this AVWAP line, and what that implies about the average long-term buyer’s position. Add a second Anchored VWAP, this time anchored to April 1st of the current financial year, in a different color. Compare the two AVWAP lines — are they close together or far apart? What does the gap between them suggest about how sentiment has shifted across the year? Repeat with NSE:ICICIBANK, anchoring to its last quarterly results date, and observe how price has behaved relative to that post-results AVWAP since. Key Takeaways Standard session VWAP (Program 3) is strictly an intraday tool and resets daily — it cannot be meaningfully used for long-term analysis. Anchored VWAP (AVWAP) solves this by letting you fix the starting point manually, making the volume-weighted average price concept usable across weeks, months, or years. Choosing the right anchor date (52-week low, financial year start, major results/budget day) is what makes AVWAP meaningful — an arbitrary anchor produces an arbitrary line. AVWAP works best as a complementary tool alongside SMA/EMA trend analysis, since it adds a volume dimension that simple moving averages do not account for. 6.5 Program 4: MACD Momentum Strategy Objective To identify trend strength and potential reversals using the Moving Average Convergence Divergence (MACD) indicator — applied to Indian stock market (NSE/BSE) charts on TradingView for long-term investing decisions. What makes MACD different from Programs 1 and 2? SMA and EMA are plotted directly on the price chart as overlays. MACD, by contrast, is an oscillator — it appears in a separate panel below the main price chart and measures the relationship between two EMAs (typically 12 and 26 period) along with a signal line and a histogram. This makes it especially useful for spotting momentum shifts and divergences that price-only overlays can miss. Steps Step 1: Go to TradingView and Open Your Stock Chart Open your browser and navigate to tradingview.com. Sign in to your account. Search for and select your NSE stock — for example, type RELIANCE in the symbol search bar and choose the listing tagged NSE (it should appear as NSE:RELIANCE). Set the timeframe to 1W (1 Week) using the timeframe selector in the top toolbar, so the chart displays weekly candles suitable for long-term analysis. Step 2: Open the Indicators Panel Click on “Indicators” in the top toolbar of the chart (shown with the fx-style icon). The “Indicators & Strategies” search panel will open. Step 3: Search and Add MACD In the search box, type MACD. From the results list, select the built-in “MACD” (Moving Average Convergence Divergence) indicator — the official one maintained by TradingView. Click on it once to add it to your chart. By default it uses the standard settings: a 12-period EMA, a 26-period EMA, and a 9-period signal line — these defaults work well for weekly NSE stock analysis and generally don’t need to be changed for this exercise. Step 4: MACD Added Below the Chart Unlike SMA/EMA, the MACD indicator does not sit on top of the price candles. Instead, it opens in a separate panel directly below your main chart. In this panel, you’ll see three components: MACD Line (typically blue) — the difference between the 12-period and 26-period EMA. Signal Line (typically orange/red) — a 9-period EMA of the MACD line itself, used to generate crossover signals. Histogram (vertical bars, usually green/red) — visually represents the gap between the MACD line and the signal line; taller bars indicate stronger momentum in that direction. You’ll also notice a horizontal zero line running through the middle of this panel — this is a key reference level used in the trading rule below. Interpretation Signal Visual Cue Meaning  MACD Line Crossing Above Signal Line Blue line crosses upward over the orange/red signal line Buy signal — momentum is turning positive  MACD Line Crossing Below Signal Line Blue line crosses downward below the orange/red signal line Sell signal — momentum is turning negative  Divergence Price makes a new high/low on the main chart, but MACD fails to make a corresponding new high/low Reversal Signal — momentum is weakening even though price is still extending, often an early warning of a trend change Understanding Divergence in the Indian context: Divergence is one of the most valuable uses of MACD for long-term NSE investors. For example, if a stock like NSE:HDFCBANK makes a new 52-week high but the MACD line makes a lower high compared to its previous peak, this is called bearish divergence — it suggests the rally is losing underlying momentum even though price looks strong, often preceding a correction. The reverse (price making a lower low while MACD makes a higher low) is bullish divergence, often seen near the end of prolonged corrections in quality stocks. Trading Rule  BUY: Enter when there is a MACD crossover above the zero line — meaning the MACD line not only crosses above the signal line, but the crossover itself occurs while the MACD line is in positive territory (above the zero line on the panel). This combination signals that momentum has turned bullish and is strong enough to be in positive territory, filtering out weaker crossover signals that happen deep in negative territory during a still-strong downtrend. Why the zero line matters: A crossover that happens below the zero line, while it can still mark a momentum shift, often happens during weak, range-bound, or recovering downtrends and tends to be less reliable for committing fresh long-term capital. Waiting for the crossover to occur above the zero line adds a layer of trend confirmation, similar in spirit to combining EMA crossovers with price position in Program 2. Combining With Earlier Programs MACD works well as a complementary momentum check alongside the trend tools from Programs 1 and 2: Use SMA 50 (Program 1) to confirm the stock is in a broad uptrend (price above the SMA). Use EMA 20/50 crossover (Program 2) to confirm short-term momentum aligns with the long-term trend. Use MACD (this chapter) as a final momentum and divergence check — particularly useful for catching early warning signs of trend exhaustion in stocks that otherwise look strong on the SMA/EMA overlays alone. Practice Exercise Open NSE:TCS on the Weekly timeframe and add the MACD indicator with default settings. Scroll through the last 2–3 years of price history and identify at least two MACD crossovers that occurred above the zero line, and one that occurred below it. Compare how price behaved after each type of crossover — did the above-zero-line crossovers tend to lead to stronger, more sustained moves? Look for any instance of bullish or bearish divergence between price and the MACD line on this chart, and note what happened to price in the weeks following that divergence. Repeat with NSE:ICICIBANK and compare the frequency and reliability of MACD signals between a banking stock and an IT stock. Key Takeaways MACD is a momentum oscillator that sits below the price chart and tracks the relationship between a fast and slow EMA, helping identify both trend strength and early signs of reversal. MACD line crossovers above/below the signal line generate basic buy/sell signals, while divergence between price and MACD often provides earlier warning of a weakening trend than price action alone. Requiring the crossover to occur above the zero line adds a useful filter, helping long-term investors avoid weaker signals that occur during shaky or recovering downtrends. MACD is most effective when used alongside trend-confirmation tools like SMA and EMA (Programs 1 and 2) rather than in isolation, especially for long-term investing decisions on NSE stocks.
